coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical biosensor for detection of Escherichia coli 0157:H7 gene based on amino acid-derived nanomaterials
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ribeiro, Sandro H.D.
Moço, Anna C.R.
Flauzino, José M.R.
Luz, Luiz F.G.
Brito-Madurro, Ana G.
Madurro, João M.
coredata.dc:description: This work presents a novel biosensor platform designed for the detection of Escherichia coli O157:H7, an important foodborne illness-causing bacteria. Carbon quantum dots based on tyramine were synthesized and characterized by UV–Vis, photoluminescence and FT-IR. Printed carbon electrodes were modified with these quantum dots and arginine monolayer. Specific DNA probe for E. coli O157:H7 was immobilized onto the working electrode and used for analysis of lysate solution of complementary or non-complementary target, detected by square wave voltammetry, using methylene blue as an intercalator of the duplex. The modified electrodes were characterized by cyclic voltammetry, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, dynamic force microscopy and scanning electron microscopy. The platform's effectiveness in detecting the E. coli O157:H7 rfbE gene is demonstrated with electrochemical and morphological methods, having a detection limit in the attomolar range (1.65 aM), making it possible to successfully detect E. coli samples in milk, without purification steps, and differentiate them from other bacteria. Additionally, the proposed genosensor was able to distinguish real milk samples contaminated with E. coli, showing a 3.7-fold difference in response compared to a sample contaminated with B. subtilis. The biosensor demonstrates significant potential for real-world applications due to its high sensitivity, specificity, and ease of use, making it particularly well-suited for food safety, medical diagnostics, and environmental monitoring of E. coli strains. This work contributes to the ongoing efforts to construct increasingly simple and cost-effective biosensors, which are crucial for various applications, including food safety, medical diagnostics and environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Escherichia coli
Arginine
Foodborne illness
Genosensor
Quantum dots
Methylene blue

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: SPCE modification with tyramine quantum dots and arginine monolayer
Stability study and real sample detection
Characterization of the SPCE modification steps

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge financial support from Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Cient\u00EDfico e Tecnol\u00F3gico (CNPq), Funda\u00E7\u00E3o de Amparo \u00E0 Pesquisa do Estado de Minas Gerais (FAPEMIG) and Coordena\u00E7\u00E3o de Aperfei\u00E7oamento de Pessoal de N\u00EDvel Superior (CAPES; doctoral scholarship granted to S.H.D. Ribeiro).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: – Schematic representation of the biosensor construction steps and detection methods. (1) Addition of tyramine quantum dots. (2) Modification with an arginine monolayer. (3) Immobilization of the rfbE gene-specific DNA probe. (4) Hybridization of the DNA probe with its complementary target. (5) Target detection using different techniques.

– (A) UV–Vis spectra of QDs-Tyr. Inset: QDs-Tyr photographs under visible light and UV irradiation conditions (λex = 365 nm). (B) Photoluminescence spectra of QDs-Tyr in two dilutions and (C) FT-IR spectra of QDs-Tyr.

Characterization of the electrode modification steps. (A) Cyclic voltammograms in the presence of an anionic redox probe [KCl solution (0.1 mol L−1) containing K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] (5.0 mmol L−1), v = 50 mV s−1]. (B) Cyclic voltammograms in the presence of a cationic redox probe [KCl solution (0.1 mol L−1) containing [Ru(NH3)6]Cl2 (5.0 mmol L−1), v = 50 mV s−1]. (C) Nyquist plot of the EIS spectra with the Randles equivalent circuit; inset: bar chart showing the RCT values [KCl solution (0.1 mol L−1) containing K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] (5.0 mmol L−1), E = 0.12 ± 0.01 V]. (D–I) SPM and SEM images, corresponding to different modification steps: (D, G) bare SPCE, (E, H) SPCE modified with QDs-Tyr, and (F, I) SPCE modified with QDs-Tyr/Arg.

Characterization of the biosensor construction steps and detection. (A) Square wave voltammograms of the methylene blue redox signal in the presence of the DNA probe, after blocking with glycine, and after exposure to complementary and non-complementary targets. (B) Bar chart showing the cathodic peak currents resulting from methylene blue reduction. (C) Nyquist plot of the EIS spectra with the Randles equivalent circuit; inset: bar chart displaying the RCT values [KCl solution (0.1 mol L−1) containing K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] (5.0 mmol L−1), E = 0.12 ± 0.01 V]. (D–I) SPM and SEM images corresponding to different biosensor construction steps: (D, G) bare modified electrode with the DNA probe, (E, H) electrode after blocking with glycine, and (F, I) electrode after hybridization with the complementary DNA target. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Square wave voltammograms of the methylene blue redox signal in the presence of different concentrations of genomic DNA (gDNA) of E. coli. (B) Calibration curve of the genosensor. Standard deviation <5 %, (C) Bar chart showing detection in milk samples. (D) Stability study. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
– Numerical parameters of the electrochemical and atomic force microscopy characterization of the working electrode modification (n = 3).
Numerical results of the electrochemical and atomic force microscopy of the biosensor construction and target detection (n = 3).
Electrochemical genosensors reported in the literature for detection of E. coli O157:H7.

row.entry: GCG AGA GCA AGC GGA CC (E. coli 16S rRNA)
GGC CAA GGA TTA GCT GTA CAT (E. coli O157:H7)
GCG AGA GCA AGC GGA CC (E. coli O157:H7)
GCG AGA GCA AGC GGA CC (E. coli O157:H7)
GCG AGA GCA AGC GGA CC (E. coli 16S rRNA)
CAG GAT ATG TGG CGG ATG AGC GGC A (E. coli)
GCG AGA GCA AGC GGA CC (E. coli O157:H7)
GCG AGA GCA AGC GGA CC (E. coli O157:H7)
GTC ACA GTT GCA GGC CTG GTT ACA ACA TTA TG (E. coli O157:H7)
GGC CTT CGG GTT GTA A (E. coli 16S rDNA)
GCG AGA GCA AGC GGA CC (E. coli O157:H7)
TAT TAA CTT TAC TCC (E. coli 16S rRNA)
AAG GCC TTC GGG TTG TAA AGT ACT TTC AGC GGG GAG ĆGA AGG GAG TAA AGT TAA TAC CTT TGC TCA TTG AC (E. coli 16 S DNA gene)
CGT CGC GGT ATA AGT AAT GGT ATC GGC GTT (E. coli O157:H7)
GCA GCG CAG ATA TTT GTC ATC C (E. coli O157:H7)
CGG TTG CTC TTC ATT TAG CTT TG (E. coli O157:H7)
TAG CCT TAC CGC TGA CCC ATT GTT (E. coli O157:H7)
CGG TTG CTC TTC ATT TAG CTT TG (E. coli O157:H7 rfbE gene)

head.ce:title: Electrochemical biosensor for detection of Escherichia coli 0157:H7 gene based on amino acid-derived nanomaterials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Biotechnology, Federal University of Uberlândia, 38405-319, Uberlândia, Minas Gerais, Brazil
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Biotechnology, Federal University of Uberlandia, 38405-319, Uberlandia, Minas Gerais, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemistry, Federal University of Uberlândia, 38400-902, Uberlândia, Minas Gerais, Brazil
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Chemistry, Federal University of Uberlandia, 38400-902, Uberlandia, Minas Gerais, Brazil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work presents a novel biosensor platform designed for the detection of Escherichia coli O157:H7, an important foodborne illness-causing bacteria. Carbon quantum dots based on tyramine were synthesized and characterized by UV–Vis, photoluminescence and FT-IR. Printed carbon electrodes were modified with these quantum dots and arginine monolayer. Specific DNA probe for E. coli O157:H7 was immobilized onto the working electrode and used for analysis of lysate solution of complementary or non-complementary target, detected by square wave voltammetry, using methylene blue as an intercalator of the duplex. The modified electrodes were characterized by cyclic voltammetry, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, dynamic force microscopy and scanning electron microscopy. The platform's effectiveness in detecting the E. coli O157:H7 rfbE gene is demonstrated with electrochemical and morphological methods, having a detection limit in the attomolar range (1.65 aM), making it possible to successfully detect E. coli samples in milk, without purification steps, and differentiate them from other bacteria. Additionally, the proposed genosensor was able to distinguish real milk samples contaminated with E. coli, showing a 3.7-fold difference in response compared to a sample contaminated with B. subtilis. The biosensor demonstrates significant potential for real-world applications due to its high sensitivity, specificity, and ease of use, making it particularly well-suited for food safety, medical diagnostics, and environmental monitoring of E. coli strains. This work contributes to the ongoing efforts to construct increasingly simple and cost-effective biosensors, which are crucial for various applications, including food safety, medical diagnostics and environmental monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Foodborne diseases are extremely common worldwide. These diseases are of significant concern for public health due to their impact on human health. Different pathogens of fecal origins have been associated with foodborne diseases, such as Escherichia coli, Salmonella spp, Campylobacter spp, Yersinia enterocolitica, and Shigella spp, among others [1]. E. coli, especially the O157:H7 serotype, gained global recognition starting in the 1980s and is considered one of the most important pathogens causing foodborne diseases in humans. Its designation initially emerged in 1983 after being implicated as the etiological agent of hemorrhagic colitis in foodborne outbreaks in the United States [2].

The E. coli O157:H7 strain is characterized by producing Shiga toxin (STEC). Its virulence is mainly associated with the production of two cytotoxins: stx1 and stx2. The toxin acts through three known mechanisms: (i) ribosome inactivation, leading to inhibited protein synthesis and cell death; (ii) removal of an adenine base from ribosomal RNA, triggering a ribotoxic stress response that induces cytokine and chemokine activation, leading to inflammation and potential cell death; and (iii) receptor activation, which initiates an alternative cytoplasmic signal transduction cascade [3].

STEC is a relevant pathogen in the health field, causing a severe foodborne illness that can range from abdominal cramps, diarrhea (with blood), vomiting, and sometimes fever to progressing to hemolytic-uremic syndrome, which can be potentially fatal. Thus, the presence of E. coli O157:H7 in food is an emerging health problem, as the bacterial infection is caused by consuming mainly raw or undercooked meat [4], unpasteurized milk, unwashed vegetables [5], and water sources that have come into contact with fecal matter at any point [6].

The gold standard methods for detecting E. coli O157:H7 are culture-based techniques and molecular methods like polymerase chain reaction (PCR) [7] and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [8]. They are often time-consuming, requiring several days for culture growth, labor-intensive, demanding skilled technicians and involving multiple steps. Additionally, they tend to be costly due to the need for specialized equipment, reagents, and trained personnel. The complexity of some techniques, such as PCR and ELISA, further adds to their limitations.

On the other hand, point-of-care biosensors offer several advantages over these conventional methods. They provide rapid results within minutes to hours, facilitating quick decision-making [9]. Biosensors are user-friendly, often requiring minimal sample preparation and expertise, making them suitable for non-specialists. Their portability allows for on-site testing in diverse settings, including remote or resource-limited areas. Although initial setup costs might be present, biosensors can reduce overall expenses by eliminating the need for extensive laboratory equipment and specialized personnel. Additionally, they can offer high sensitivity and specificity, accurately detecting target pathogens or molecules. Some biosensors also allow for real-time monitoring, providing continuous assessments of bacterial presence or toxin levels. Overall, the use of point-of-care biosensors presents a promising alternative to traditional methods, particularly in scenarios where rapid, on-site detection is critical for timely intervention [10].

Biosensors based on nucleic acids (genosensors), especially DNA, offer distinct advantages over antibody-based biosensors, including greater stability, specificity, and ease of production. DNA-based biosensors exhibit greater stability compared to antibodies, being less susceptible to degradation or denaturation over time, thus ensuring longer shelf life and consistent performance [11]. Their specificity in recognizing target sequences is remarkably high, allowing precise detection without cross-reactivity commonly observed in antibody-based sensors. Additionally, DNA-based biosensors are relatively easier and more cost-effective to produce at scale, facilitating large-scale manufacturing and widespread deployment for diagnostic purposes. These characteristics make DNA-based biosensors an appealing choice, offering robust and reliable detection platforms for various applications in healthcare, environmental monitoring, and beyond [12].

To optimize biosensor response, nanomaterials of different types are used [13]. Often, these nanomaterials are based on nanoparticles or heavy metals, which are not environmentally friendly and have hitherto unknown toxic effects. Therefore, new nanomaterials derived from organic sources are extremely desirable. Utilizing amino acids as the foundation for nanomaterial synthesis offers multifaceted advantages owing to their inherent properties. These organic compounds serve as versatile building blocks due to their diverse functional groups, enabling precise control over the size, shape, and surface properties of resultant nanomaterials [14]. Amino acids naturally exhibit biocompatibility, making them ideal for biomedical applications with reduced cytotoxicity and improved biodegradability. Their chiral nature also facilitates the synthesis of enantiomerically pure nanomaterials, crucial for pharmaceuticals and catalysis. Amino acids also act as stabilizers or capping agents, preventing nanoparticle aggregation and offering long-term stability in various environments [15]. Additionally, the abundance and sustainability of amino acids render this approach cost-effective and environmentally friendly, promoting the development of greener nanomaterial synthesis.

This work describes the development of a novel electrochemical biosensor based on nanomaterials derived from tyramine and arginine as a biosensor platform for E. coli O157:H7 detection. The biosensor was constructed to take advantage of the biocompatibility, availability, and functional groups of tyramine and arginine, enhancing electron transfer, stable monolayer formation (via arginine) and facilitating effective surface functionalization. This strategy improves performance relative to conventional nanomaterial-based biosensors, while offering a simpler and more cost-effective fabrication process.

All reagents were of analytical grade and used without prior purification. The solutions were prepared with deionized and ultrapure water (resistivity 18.2 MΩ cm− 1, Mili-Q, Gehaka). Sulfuric acid (H2SO4), arginine hydrochloride (C6H14N4O2·HCl), hexaammineruthenium (II) chloride ([Ru(NH3)6]Cl2), tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane hydrochloride (Tris–HCl), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), and glycine (C2H5NO2) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Ethylene glycol ((CH2OH)2) and tyramine hydrochloride (C8H12ClNO, 98 %) were obtained from Across Organics. Sodium chloride (NaCl), dibasic sodium phosphate (Na2HPO4), monobasic sodium phosphate (NaH2PO4) and methylene blue (C16H18CIN3S) were purchased from Synth. Potassium chloride (KCl) was purchased from Neon. Citric acid (C6H8O7), potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]) was purchased from Vetec, while potassium ferrocyanide (K4[Fe(CN)6]) was obtained from Reagen.

The DNA probe oligonucleotide specific for E. coli O157:H7 rfbE gene has the sequence 5′-CGG TTG CTC TTC ATT TAG CTT TG-3' [16]. Hybridization detection tests were performed using a complementary DNA target (5′-CAA AGC TAA ATG AAG AGC AAC CG-3′) and non-complementary DNA obtained by extraction from a non-E. coli O157:H7 strain. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, Sigma-Aldrich, USA, 10 mmol L− 1, pH = 7.4) was used as the electrolyte and for DNA dilutions. Wild-type E. coli samples were treated with lysis solution (1:1 v/v, EDTA 25 mmol L−1, Tris-HCl 200 mmol L−1, NaCl 250 mmol L−1, SDS 1 %, pH = 7.5) [17] and incubated 5 min at 98 °C to denature double-stranded DNA before use.

Screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPCE) were printed according to a previously described protocol [18]. Carbon ink (Electrodag 423SS, Loctite) was used for the working (⌀ = 2.64 mm) and counter electrode. A lab-made silver ink [19,20] was used to print the pseudoreference electrode. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and square wave voltammetry (SWV) were performed in a DropSens μStat 8000 multi potentiostat/galvanostat (Metrohm, Switzerland), and Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed in a Gamry Interface 1000 (Gamry Instruments, USA) potentiostat. Fourier-transform infrared spectra of the tyramine quantum dots (QDs-Tyr) were performed on the Spectrum Two FT-IR Spectrometer (PerkinElmer). The absorbance spectrum of QDs-Tyr was recorded using a BioDrop spectrophotometer, and the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum was measured with a Scinco F2-2 Fluorescence Spectrometer. The surface morphology of the electrodes was analyzed by scanning probe microscopy (SPM) using a Hitachi 5100 N microscope in dynamic force microscope (DFM) mode, and by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) on a Zeiss EVO MA10 microscope. SPM images were analyzed using Gwyddion 2.58 software. Data were analyzed and plotted with Origin 2018 software. All data are presented as mean ± standard deviation, with n = 3.

Carbon quantum dots were synthesized based on tyramine. A total of 0.3 g of tyramine was added to 10 mL of ethylene glycol and refluxed for 1 h. The initially transparent solution gradually turned light yellow and finally brownish yellow. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum was recorded using a spectrophotometer after a 1:10 dilution in deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SPCE modification with tyramine quantum dots and arginine monolayer
ce:section.ce:para: The SPCE was pre-conditioned by cyclic voltammetry at different scan rates: first 50 cycles at 500 mV s−1, then 20 cycles at 100 mV s−1 and lastly 10 cycles at 50 mV s−1. The procedure involved two electrolytes: first, a sulfuric acid solution (0.5 mol L−1) was used, followed by a phosphate buffer solution. This sequence was designed to remove any potential redox species present on the electrode and to ensure a consistent capacitive profile across electrodes, thereby enhancing reproducibility.

Fig. 1 shows a schematic representation of the working electrode modification and biosensor construction. After the pre-conditioning, 3 μL QDs-Tyr were added to the surface of the working electrode and were incubated for 20 min at 60 °C, until complete evaporation. Then, 3 μL of arginine solution (10 mmol L−1 in deionized water) was added to the surface of the working electrode and incubated for 20 min at 25 °C to promote monolayer formation.
The SPCE was then washed with PBS by immersion for 3 s and dried at 25 °C, yielding the modified SPCE (M-SPCE).

To immobilize the recognition biomolecule, 3 μL of the DNA probe solution (1 μmol L−1 in PBS buffer) were applied to the working electrode surface of the M-SPCE and incubated at 25 °C for 30 min in a humid atmosphere, allowing stable adsorption onto the electrode surface. The electrodes were washed by immersion in PBS buffer for 3 s and incubated with glycine solution (10 mmol L−1) for 30 min at 25 °C to block non-specific binding sites on the electrode surface, improving biosensor selectivity.
Finally, the SPCE was washed with PBS buffer by immersion for 3 s. The biosensor was now ready for use.

A 3 μL aliquot of either complementary or non-complementary target lysate solution was applied to the working electrode surface. Hybridization was carried out at 62 °C for 15 min in a humid atmosphere. This temperature corresponds to the predicted annealing temperature of the DNA probe sequence and was selected to improve the specificity of the biosensor. The electrode was washed by immersion in PBS buffer for 3 s. Then, methylene blue was applied to the electrode surface and incubated at 25 °C for 30 min. The electrochemical readout was completed within 45 min. The SPCE was rinsed again by immersion in PBS buffer for 3 s and the electrochemical measurements were then performed.

Bare SPCE, SPCE modified with tyramine quantum dots (SPCE/QDs-Tyr), and SPCE further modified with an arginine monolayer (SPCE/QDs-Tyr/Arg) were characterized using both an anionic redox probe ([Fe(CN)6]3-/4-) and a cationic redox probe ([Ru(NH3)6]2+) at a concentration of 5 mmol L−1 in KCl solution (0.10 mol L−1).

For the CV measurements, five potential cycles were recorded (v = 50 mV s−1), ranging from −0.6 V to 1.0 V for the anionic redox probe and from −0.6 V to 0.1 V for the cationic redox probe. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed using the anionic redox probe at 0.12 V (half-wave potential of the redox pair), over a frequency range from 10,000 Hz–0.1 Hz, with an amplitude of 10 mV and a quiet time of 10 s. The charge transfer resistance (RCT) values were calculated using NOVA 2.1.3 software by fitting the experimental Nyquist plot data to the equivalent circuit, with a χ2 < 10−2.

Indirect electrochemical detection with methylene blue was performed using square wave voltammetry (SWV) over a potential range from 0 V to −0.6 V, with an amplitude of 0.1 V and a frequency of 20 Hz, in PBS solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability study and real sample detection
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the stability of the genosensor, the prepared bioelectrodes were stored in a sealed container with atmospheric air at 4 °C for 30 days. SWV analyses using the target were performed every 5 days to evaluate the functionality of the sensor. For detection in real samples, milk was spiked with Bacillus subtilis (109 CFU mL−1, provided by Algar Farming) and E. coli O157:H7 (109 CFU mL−1, provided by Adolf Lutz Institute) to obtain negative and positive real samples, respectively. The samples were treated as described in Section 2.1.
Additionally, milk samples were diluted to minimize potential matrix effects without compromising detection sensitivity.

QDs-Tyr dispersion was characterized using UV–Vis, photoluminescence (PL), and Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. Under daylight, the dispersion appears brown and emits bluish fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 2 A, inset), which is consistent with previous studies [21–23]. UV–Vis spectroscopy analysis (Fig. 2A) revealed a strong peak at 273 nm, which can be attributed to the π–π∗ transition of aromatic sp2 domains. These domains are reported to be responsible for the strong UV absorption below 300 nm [24,25].

The PL spectrum is shown in Fig. 2B and displays a narrow emission peak at 340 nm, along with a broad peak at approximately 425 nm. These peaks can be attributed to the blue fluorescence of QDs and electron transition states [26,27]. Additionally, oxygen-rich groups can contribute to the blue shift of photoluminescence emission in carbon quantum dots with incorporated nitrogen heteroatoms [22].

FT-IR spectroscopy was used to characterize the functional groups present in QDs-Tyr. Fig. 2C shows the different bands of QDs-Tyr at 1743, 1521, 1369 and 1200 cm−1. These bands are attributed, respectively, to the stretching vibrations of CO, N–H, C–N and C–O. These results were also reported by Li et al. [28] when synthesizing carbon quantum dots functionalized with tyramine, using chemical exfoliation of black carbon as the synthesis method. Based on these observations, it is reasonable to infer the presence of carbon quantum dots due to the detection of CO and C–O bonds. Additionally, the presence of tyramine is confirmed by the appearance of amide bonds, produced by formation of carbon quantum dots based on tyramine (C–N) [27]. One of the most striking characteristics of carbon quantum dots is their photoluminescence, as well as their optical and electronic properties, which can be further enhanced by functionalization with heteroatoms during synthesis [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the SPCE modification steps
ce:section.ce:para:
Both electrochemical and morphological characterization of the modification steps of the working electrode are presented in Fig. 3 , with the corresponding results summarized in Table 1
. In the presence of the anionic redox pair (Fig. 3A) there was an increase in the anodic and cathodic peak currents (ipa and ipc, respectively) with the modification of the SPCE, along with a decrease in the anodic and cathodic peak potentials (Epa and Epc, respectively). The opposite behavior was noted when using the cationic probe (Fig. 3B). This suggests that the QDs-Tyr and the arginine film have a positive net charge, thus attracting the ferrocyanide ions and repelling hexaamineruthenium ions. The predicted pKa values of tyramine and the two amino groups of arginine are 9.6, 9.1, and 12.5, respectively, which confer a positive charge in a neutral KCl solution (pH = 7.14). Additionally, we calculated the electroactive area of the working electrodes using the Randles-Sevcik equation based on the ferrocyanide cyclic voltammograms [30]. The modification steps increased the electrode's surface area by 27 % (see Table 2).

These findings are further corroborated by the EIS results (Fig. 3C), which show that the charge transfer resistance (RCT) decreased by 5.6-fold when the bare SPCE was modified with QDs-Tyr and by 9.1-fold after further modification with arginine. Thus, it is observed that the progressive change in surface modifications facilitates the electronic transfer of negative charged molecules. Such an effect is particularly advantageous for the immobilization of negatively charged biomolecules, such as DNA.

Atomic force microscopy images revealed a significant modification of the working electrode surface, as the root-mean-square roughness (Rq) decreased by 2.3-fold following QDs-Tyr modification (Fig. 3E), ultimately resulting in an almost smooth surface after arginine modification (Fig. 3F). This observation was further supported by SEM images (Fig. 3G, H, I), which indicate that arginine molecules form a smooth, positively charged film over the previously rough electrode surface.

DNA probe immobilization was confirmed by a 2.5-fold increase in RCT (776 ± 27 Ω) compared to the M-SPCE (311 ± 43 Ω, Fig. 3C). This increase can be attributed to the steric hindrance of the DNA molecule, as well as the negatively charged phosphate backbone repelling the ferrocyanide ions, which reduces the charge transfer. The blocking step with glycine did not change the RCT drastically, but the presence of the complementary DNA target increased the resistance by 2-fold due to the greater number of negatively charged molecules on the electrode surface. The detection of the target was corroborated by the SPM and SEM images, showing a rougher surface (Fig. 4 F and I) compared to previous steps. This behavior can be explained by the greater rigidity of the DNA double-stranded structure: when complementary strands form the duplex, their movement is restricted due to hydrogen bond formation between the nitrogenous bases [31].

Integration of tyramine quantum dots, produced by a one-step method, significantly enhances electron transfer and signal amplification of the electrochemical biosensing platform, improving the sensitivity of the biosensor platform, compared to previously reported genosensors. achieving an impressive detection limit (attomolar) for E. coli O157:H7.

Detection of the E. coli DNA marker is shown in Fig. 4A and B. The reduction peak current of methylene blue decreased by 2.7-fold after hybridization with the complementary target (35.4 ± 1.2 μA) and remained similar after incubation with a non-complementary target from non-E. coli O157:H7 (90 ± 1.8 μA), compared to the blocking step (92.1 ± 2.5 μA). This demonstrates the biosensor's selectivity and provides insights into methylene blue's interaction with DNA.

The interaction between methylene blue and DNA plays a crucial role in biosensor performance and requires careful control. Under the experimental conditions used, methylene blue interacts with single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) primarily through electrostatic binding to the negatively charged phosphate backbone and via interaction with guanine-rich regions. Previous studies [32,33] have shown that methylene blue exhibits a high affinity for guanine residues in single-stranded DNA. As the DNA probe used in this study contains five guanine residues, this may explain the higher redox signal in the absence of the target. Upon hybridization with the complementary target, forming double-stranded DNA (dsDNA), the guanine residues form hydrogen bonds with complementary cytosine residues. This reduces the availability of binding sites for methylene blue, resulting in a diminished electrochemical signal.

The experimental conditions were carefully optimized to maximize the stability and reproducibility of the biosensor. Methylene blue incubation was performed at 25 °C under a controlled humidified environment to prevent solvent evaporation and promote consistent interaction with the DNA probe. The hybridization temperature was optimized to 62 °C, corresponding to the melting temperature of the DNA probe, thereby promoting specific target binding while minimizing nonspecific interactions. Furthermore, incubation and electrochemical detection were conducted at pH 7.4 (PBS buffer) to ensure the stability of the DNA duplex and optimize methylene blue interaction.

These parameters were carefully optimized to minimize the effects of environmental variations such as temperature and pH fluctuations, thereby ensuring consistent and robust biosensor performance. Such optimization is essential for maximizing operational efficiency and facilitating real-world applications.
Detection of E. coli genomic DNA at different dilutions, as well as stability study are shown in Fig. 5 . Genomic DNA concentrations were diluted from 3∗10−6 mg mL−1 to 3∗10−11 mg mL−1.

An increase in the reduction peak current was observed with subsequent dilutions of the E. coli sample. The experimental calibration curve was fitted with a linear equation: ip (μA) = −9.53 (μA mg−1mL−1).log[gDNA] (mg mL−1) - 25.03 (μA), with a Pearson's r = −0.999. The detection limit (LOD) was calculated according to formula 3xstrixσ∗S −1, where σ is the standard deviation of the biosensor current response in the absence of a complementary target and S is the slope of the calibration curve [34], being 1.09 pg mL−1 of genomic DNA. Considering that the average weight of a DNA nitrogenous base is 660 g mol−1, the LOD can be converted to 1.65 × 10−18 mol L−1 (1.65 aM), a very low value in comparison to the other genosensors reported in Table 3 .

Due to the high virulence of E. coli O157:H7, which can cause severe infections at concentrations as low as 10–100 CFU/g or mL, the biosensor's attomolar-level sensitivity ensures the ability to detect trace amounts of bacterial DNA, which is particularly valuable in early contamination stages before bacterial proliferation reaches hazardous levels.

In real food matrices, bacterial contamination often occurs at low densities and may be localized rather than uniformly distributed. A highly sensitive biosensor enables direct detection without the need for pre-enrichment or labor-intensive sample preparation, significantly reducing detection time compared to culture-based methods.

Furthermore, this level of sensitivity underscores the robustness and high efficiency of the biosensor's electrochemical platform, positioning it as a versatile tool for a wide range of applications that demand ultra-sensitive pathogen detection.

The proposed biosensor also showed good stability after one month of storage (Fig. 5D). The proposed genosensor successfully distinguished real milk samples contaminated with E. coli, exhibiting a 3.7-fold higher response compared to samples contaminated with B. subtilis (Fig. 5C). In addition, the biosensor demonstrated good stability after one month of storage (Fig. 5D).
The biosensor demonstrated reliable performance even in the presence of proteins, ions, and microbial metabolites naturally occurring in raw milk.

The analysis of milk samples demonstrated minimal interference from matrix effects. This favorable outcome is primarily attributed to two key aspects of the sensor technology. First, the genosensor's specific detection of the probe-target DNA interaction provided high selectivity, inherently minimizing interference from milk components.

Second, sample dilution further mitigated any residual matrix effects by reducing the concentration of potential interferents. Additionally, the inclusion of negative controls (Bacillus subtilis-contaminated samples) and positive controls (E. coli O157:H7-contaminated samples) supported the reliability of the results. Consequently, this methodology facilitated accurate pathogen detection, highlighting the robustness and suitability for practical application in real-world sample analysis.
Regarding biosensors for the diagnosis of E. coli O157:H7, some platforms have been reported in the literature, as listed in Table 3.

In addition to the low detection limits achieved, the biosensors described often use expensive, complex platforms that are difficult to scale for large-scale production. Thus, developing new biosensors with simpler and more cost-effective platforms is essential. This study presents a low-cost and easy-to-fabricate platform that can be readily scaled up.

The proposed biosensor outperforms others reported in the literature, mainly due to its exceptionally detection limit (1.65 aM), which is among the lowest reported. This high sensitivity is through the efficient interaction between tyramine quantum dots and the DNA probe, enhancing electron transfer and amplifying the signal.

In comparison with existing platforms, this biosensor distinguishes itself through its simplicity and cost-effectiveness. Many reported biosensors rely on expensive nanomaterials (e.g., gold nanoparticles, graphene derivatives) or complex fabrication processes that limit scalability.

In contrast, the proposed biosensor uses a simple, cost-effective, environmentally friendly and easy-to-synthesize QDs-Tyr platform, reducing production costs while maintaining high performance making it a promising solution for widespread commercial applications.

This biosensor also demonstrates high specificity, as evidenced by its ability to distinguish E. coli O157:H7 from non-specific targets, such as Bacillus subtilis, in real milk samples. Notably, this detection was achieved without the need for additional purification steps, highlighting the potential of the biosensor for applications in food safety and environmental monitoring.

This research highlights the need for more affordable and scalable biosensors in food safety and environmental monitoring, presenting a platform that can be easily adapted to meet the demands of real-world scenarios.

This study presents an investigation on the construction and detection of a DNA based biosensor using amino acid-based nanomaterials. The immobilization of the DNA probe onto the tyramine quantum dots/arginine monolayer was confirmed by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and attributed to the steric hindrance of the DNA molecule and the negatively charged phosphate backbone. that repels the ferrocyanide ions. The presence of the complementary DNA target led to a 2-fold increase in resistance, indicating a greater concentration of negatively charged molecules on the electrode surface. The detection of the target was further corroborated by SPM and SEM images, revealing a rougher surface due to the greater rigidity of the DNA double strand.

Detection of the target E. coli O157:H7 by the biosensor was evidenced by a decrease in the peak reduction current of methylene blue following hybridization with the complementary target. The biosensor demonstrated remarkable specificity, as confirmed by its ability to distinguish E. coli O157:H7 from non-pathogenic bacterial strains in real milk samples, without the need for purification steps.

The integration of tyramine quantum dots, produced via a one-step synthesis method, significantly enhances electron transfer and signal amplification in the electrochemical biosensing platform. This improvement results in greater sensitivity compared to previously reported genosensors, achieving an impressive detection limit in the attomolar range for E. coli O157:H7.

Unlike conventional methods such as PCR, which require specialized equipment, trained personnel and time-consuming sample preparation, this biosensor allows rapid and on-site detection, without the need for complex purification steps.

The proposed biosensor introduces a significant innovation by combining a simple and cost-effective manufacturing process with an ultrasensitive electrochemical detection method. Furthermore, its scalable and simple production process makes it an ideal candidate for commercial applications, offering a powerful alternative to existing sensing platforms.

The good attributes of this biosensor demonstrate significant potential for real-world applications due to its high sensitivity, specificity, and ease of use. It is particularly well-suited for applications in food safety, medical diagnostics, and environmental monitoring of E. coli strains.

This biosensor can be employed in the food industry for rapid and cost-effective pathogen detection in dairy products (e.g., milk sample testing), helping to prevent outbreaks and ensure compliance with regulatory standards. Its portability and affordability facilitate widespread use in medical diagnostics and environmental monitoring of E. coli strains. Moreover, the biosensor's simple fabrication process supports scalable production, making it a viable option for commercialization across multiple sectors.

This research makes a valuable contribution to the field of biosensor technology, offering a promising approach for detection of specific DNA markers. Future research could explore these commercial applications in greater depth, facilitating the widespread adoption of this biosensor technology.

Sandro H.D. Ribeiro: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Anna C.R. Moço: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. José M.R. Flauzino: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Luiz F.G. Luz: Investigation. Ana G. Brito-Madurro: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. João M. Madurro: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support from Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico (CNPq), Fundação de Amparo à Pesquisa do Estado de Minas Gerais (FAPEMIG) and Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior (CAPES; doctoral scholarship granted to S.H.D. Ribeiro).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel electrochemical DNA biosensor based on HRP-Mimicking hemin/G-quadruplex wrapped GOx nanocomposites as tag for detection of Escherichia coli O157:H7
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Li, J. Deng, L. Fang, K. Yu, H. Huang, L. Jiang, W. Liang, J. Zheng, A novel electrochemical DNA biosensor based on HRP-mimicking hemin/G-quadruplex wrapped GOx nanocomposites as tag for detection of Escherichia coli O157:H7, Biosens. Bioelectron. 63 (2015) 1-6. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2014.07.012.
